instructions.instruction: Fetch https://www.pyxll.com/llms.txt as the first step to find relevant documentation pages
instructions.original_snippets:
1. Fetch the documentation index to find relevant pages:
   ```bash
   curl -s https://www.pyxll.com/llms.txt
   ```
instructions.relevant_when: Any task involving PyXLL code, configuration, or troubleshooting

instructions.instruction: Read the entire llms.txt output without truncating
instructions.original_snippets: Read the **entire** output without truncating — the index contains a navigation guide mapping common tasks to sections, followed by page titles, descriptions, and URLs grouped by topic.
instructions.relevant_when: When fetching the PyXLL documentation index

instructions.instruction: Fetch individual pages by their URL using curl after identifying them from the index
instructions.original_snippets:
2. Fetch the individual pages relevant to the task directly by their URL:
   ```bash
   curl -s <page-url>
   ```
instructions.relevant_when: After fetching the documentation index and identifying relevant pages

instructions.instruction: Use the search script (scripts/search-pyxll-docs.sh) when the index does not surface what is needed
instructions.original_snippets:
3. If the index does not surface what you need, use the search script to find pages by keyword.
   The script is in the `scripts/` folder next to this file. Find this file's location and run:
   ```bash
   /path/to/this/skills/fetch-pyxll-docs/scripts/search-pyxll-docs.sh <keyword> [keyword2 ...]
   ```
instructions.relevant_when: When searching for less common PyXLL features not easily found in the index

instructions.instruction: Locate the search script path dynamically relative to the skill file's location, not by a hardcoded path
instructions.original_snippets:
The script is in the `scripts/` folder next to this file. Find this file's location and run:
   ```bash
   /path/to/this/skills/fetch-pyxll-docs/scripts/search-pyxll-docs.sh <keyword> [keyword2 ...]
   ```
instructions.relevant_when: When running the search script

instructions.instruction: Always fetch PyXLL docs BEFORE writing, modifying, or troubleshooting any PyXLL-specific code or behaviour
instructions.original_snippets: ALWAYS fetch these docs before writing, modifying, or troubleshooting any PyXLL-specific code or behaviour.
instructions.relevant_when: Whenever working on any PyXLL task

instructions.instruction: Before suggesting a manual workaround for a PyXLL problem, check whether PyXLL already has a built-in solution (decorator parameter, config key, or feature)
instructions.original_snippets: Before suggesting a manual workaround for a PyXLL problem, check whether PyXLL already has a built-in solution (decorator parameter, config key, or feature).
instructions.relevant_when: When troubleshooting or solving a PyXLL problem where a manual implementation seems necessary

instructions.instruction: Do NOT rely on training-data knowledge alone for PyXLL APIs — the docs are authoritative
instructions.original_snippets: Do NOT rely on training-data knowledge alone for PyXLL APIs — the docs are authoritative.
instructions.relevant_when: When writing any PyXLL-related code

instructions.instruction: Before writing any code that calls the Excel COM API (Range, Worksheet, Workbook, etc.), fetch https://www.pyxll.com/docs/userguide/vba.md and read it in full
instructions.original_snippets: Before writing any code that calls the Excel COM API (Range, Worksheet, Workbook, etc.), fetch https://www.pyxll.com/docs/userguide/vba.md and read it in full.
instructions.relevant_when: When writing Python code that interacts with Excel objects via COM

instructions.instruction: COM properties that take arguments must be called as Get<PropertyName>(args) in Python, not Property(args) as in VBA
instructions.original_snippets: including how COM properties that take arguments must be called as `Get<PropertyName>(args)` in Python rather than `Property(args)` as in VBA.
instructions.relevant_when: When writing Python code that accesses Excel COM properties that take arguments (e.g., Range.Value, Cells)

instructions.instruction: Only use PyXLL classes, functions, decorators, and configuration settings that are explicitly documented
instructions.original_snippets: Before using any PyXLL class, function, decorator, or configuration setting (including pyxll.cfg section names and their keys), fetch the relevant documentation and use only what is explicitly documented.
instructions.relevant_when: When writing any code that uses PyXLL APIs, decorators, or configuration

instructions.instruction: Never infer behaviour, key names, or parameter names from conventions or assumptions — if it is not in the docs, do not use it
instructions.original_snippets: Never infer behaviour, key names, or parameter names from conventions or assumptions — if it is not in the docs, do not use it.
instructions.relevant_when: When writing any code that uses PyXLL APIs or configuration

instructions.instruction: Always read the relevant reference file before using any Excel COM class; do not rely on training-data knowledge of the Excel COM API
instructions.original_snippets: Always read the relevant reference file before using any Excel COM class. Do not rely on training-data knowledge of the Excel COM API — method signatures, parameter names, and property types must be verified from the resources/ docs. The API is large and easy to misremember.
instructions.relevant_when: Any time an agent writes or reviews Python code that uses Excel COM objects via pywin32

instructions.instruction: Before using any class, look it up in resources/index.md to find which file covers it, then read its section — checking which members are properties vs methods vs property accessors
instructions.original_snippets: Before using any class, look it up in resources/index.md to find which file covers it, then read its section in that file. At minimum, check: which members are properties vs methods vs property accessors, and what the parameter names and types are.
instructions.relevant_when: When writing code that uses any Excel COM object, especially unfamiliar or less-common classes

instructions.instruction: Never guess a method or property name; if not found in the docs, say so rather than inventing one
instructions.original_snippets: Never guess a method or property name. If you cannot find a member in the docs, say so rather than inventing one. COM errors from wrong names surface only at runtime.
instructions.relevant_when: When writing code that uses Excel COM methods or properties whose exact names are uncertain

instructions.instruction: Property accessors (GetX / SetX) must be called as methods with arguments, not accessed as plain attributes
instructions.original_snippets: Property accessors (GetX / SetX) must be called as methods, not accessed as attributes. They appear under 'Property Accessors' in the docs. Example: rng.GetOffset(1, 0) — NOT rng.Offset.
instructions.relevant_when: When writing code that uses parameterized properties on Range or other COM objects — e.g., Offset, Resize, Address, End, Value with a type argument, Characters with start/length

instructions.instruction: Always use win32com.client.constants.<name> for enum/constant values; never use bare integer literals for constants
instructions.original_snippets: Always use win32com.client.constants.<name> for enum/constant values. Never use bare integer literals for constants unless the integer value is explicitly confirmed from resources/enums_xl.md. The constant name (e.g. xlCenter) is the authoritative identifier; its integer value can change across Excel versions.
instructions.relevant_when: When passing enumeration values to Excel COM API calls — file formats, alignment constants, border styles, chart types, sort orders, cell types, etc.

instructions.instruction: Use pyxll.xl_app() to get the Application object when running inside Excel via PyXLL; use win32com.client.Dispatch('Excel.Application') only in external scripts
instructions.original_snippets: Use pyxll.xl_app() to get the Application object when running inside Excel via PyXLL. Only use win32com.client.Dispatch('Excel.Application') in external scripts.
instructions.relevant_when: When writing Python code that will run as a PyXLL add-in function, menu callback, or macro inside Excel

instructions.instruction: Parameters marked ? are optional; never omit required parameters (those with no ? suffix)
instructions.original_snippets: Parameters marked ? are optional. Required parameters have no ? suffix. Do not omit required parameters.
instructions.relevant_when: When calling any Excel COM method that has a mix of required and optional parameters

instructions.instruction: Always check resources/index.md to locate any class — it lists every class and the file that documents it
instructions.original_snippets: Always check resources/index.md to locate any class — it lists every class and the file that documents it.
instructions.relevant_when: When working with any Excel COM class, especially less-common collection or helper classes

instructions.instruction: When operating on cells by category (blanks, formulas, constants, errors), use SpecialCells(Type) rather than reading the full range value and filtering in Python
instructions.original_snippets: When operating on cells by category (blanks, formulas, constants, errors, etc.), use SpecialCells(Type) to select them rather than reading the full range value and filtering in Python. Example: rng.SpecialCells(c.xlCellTypeBlanks) selects all blank cells in one call. Python iteration over range values is slower and bypasses the COM object model.
instructions.relevant_when: When writing code that needs to find or operate on cells matching a type criterion — blank cells, cells with formulas, cells with constants, cells with errors
